## [00:00 - 03:06]

In this lesson, you will learn one of my best hacks for international selling on Shopify, which is related to the current converter. A lot of you might be already selling internationally, and one of the biggest challenges is being able to show pricing in the local currency. There are a lot of Shopify currency converter apps, but they're all doing the same thing. They're only just converting on the product page, but they don't convert on the checkout page because people are still going to see, for example, taking Australian Shopify owners as an example, they will always show AUD rather than the local currency from the customer side. So the benefit of having local currency, there are two things in my mind. One is obviously that we increase your conversion because people are not getting charged the Australian dollar amount, for example, in our instance. And the second thing is you're not going to lose money on the currency exchange because you can keep, you can just make money in the local currency and keep it there and convert it back to your currency whenever it makes sense. So when people come to your site and pick up from overseas and they transact, basically the currency get converted on the fly on spot. You're not getting the best exchange rate. And besides, international credit cards often come with a very high merchant facility fee. So those are the downside of people transacting in your local currency. So like for example, July, we have a Singapore site and everything is in Singapore dollar and we keep the money in our Singapore bank account. We choose when to convert back to AUD and we won't do it straight away. We only do it when the currency exchange makes sense. When you're selling internationally, there are not be two ways when you're doing on Shopify. One is using an app or doing auto currency converter. When you're checking out, customers will still see your local currency, your local currency, not their currency. And so it might be a bad at customer experience. So we have a better way to do. ## [03:06 - 06:07]

Check out. As you can see in my card page, it says SGD $295. So that's the local currency in Singapore. So how we achieve that? Basically, what we did is that we set up another store, another Shopify store in Singapore, sign up in Shopify Singapore, and making sure that the currency is SGD, the address is in Singapore, so that when people come into our website and they choose Singapore, it's actually in SGD, not AUD anymore. So taking us as example, you have to set up an entity in Singapore, but it's very, very easy to do. You just find an agent that set up a company for you there and the bank accounts, and then you can just set up a Shopify store there. And the backend is a little bit more complicated. If you don't have a custom build backend, you're going to have two Shopify backend. I just have to manage individually. It's not the best solution, but it's the best solution for now. So for anyone who are thinking about opening another store in a different country where they can get local currency, I think there are a few things I need to consider. One is where are your most customers based? If most of your customers are based in Canada, it makes a lot of sense for you to set up a store in Canada. Or where is the market that you want to enter into your very, very serious about? If you're very, very serious about the Mexican market, you might want to set up a store over there so that you can get closer to the audience. For example, if you are located in Australia and most of your customers are located in the US, it doesn't make sense for you to sell products in AUD, even if you have a currency converter. So you definitely want to set up an entity in the US and have a bank account there and have your Shopify store set up over there. If your business is growing rapidly and you want to have a good presence locally in different markets, you also want to have a dedicated store and with local currency. That's the best way to localize and that's the best way to increase your local conversion over time. Ultimately, it's how local you want to be. And we want to be as local as possible when we enter into a market and we want to be able to be as close as possible to the customers. So the actual item for this lesson is to decide whether the off-the-shelf currency converter on Shopify makes sense to you or if you want to actually open a store with local currency in another country.
